content.full_text:
TO:         CENTRAL FILE

THRU:       J. PRITCHARD

FROM:       Stein

RE:         Code §§ and Regulations Referenced:

            §4305         (§2013(a))

            §4202


JULY 6, 1976


MR. Jerry H. Stein, Treasurer
Citizens For District Elections
3633 Colorado Street
Long Beach, California 90814

Dear Mr. Stein:

This letter is in response to your letter of June 24, 1976, enclosing
two mailings sent out by the Citizens for District Elections.

As the proponent of a city ballot measure, the Citizens for District
Elections must file a campaign disclosure report with the Long Beach
City Clerk within 65 days after the measure qualifies for the ballot
or, if the measure fails to qualify, within 65 days after the final
date for circulation of petitions. In the proponent's statement, you
as treasurer of the group, must report all receipts and expenditures
in connection with the effort to qualify the measure. If your committee has never filed a campaign statement, the period covered by
your proponent's statement should begin January 7, 1975 and end on
the 58th day after the measure qualifies or the 58th day after the
final date for circulation of petitions if the measure does not
qualify. If the measure qualifies and Citizens for District Elections
subsequently collects contributions of $500.00 or more in a calendar
year, you will be required to file campaign statements before and
after the November 2, 1976 election. I've enclosed a schedule showing
the dates on which the reports must be filed with the Long Beach City
Clerk.

Your letter referred to mailings sent out during the qualification drive.
If one uses postage stamps in sending 200 or more pieces of identical
or nearly identical mail, the sender must be identified by name and
address on the outside of the mailing and on at least one of the enclosures; if the sender uses a bulk rate permit or postage meter,
however, the Political Reform Act does not require identification of
the sender.

One is only required to send copies of mass mailings in support of
or in opposition to a state candidate or state measure to the Fair
Political Practices Commission. Therefore, in the future, it will not
be necessary for you to send copies of your mailings to the Commission.

Elections Code Section 11707, not part of the Act, provides that the
printer and publisher of all printed matter referring to an election must
be identified by name and address on the mailing. Enforcement of Section

(10)

"re: Jersey N. Klein                    - 2 -                           July 6, 1976


11707 in connection with city elections is the responsibility of the city
attorney and questions relative to the requirement should be directed to
him or her.

Enclosed are materials regarding mass mailings and the campaign disclosure
provisions of the Act. If you have any questions regarding your reporting
obligations, you may contact the city clerk's office or our staff.

Sincerely,


                                          Nan Hambleton
                                          Special Compliance Representative

Mi/gh

cc: Gally Sandmayer
    Office of the City Clerk
    205 West Broadway
    Long Beach, California 90802"

CITIZENS FOR DISTRICT ELECTIONS
3633 COLORADO STREET
LONG BEACH, CALIF. 90814

JUNE 24, 1976


FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. BOX 807
SACRAMENTO, CALIF. 95814

SIRS:

WE WERE ADVISED BY THE LOCAL FILING OFFICER, THE CITY CLERK OF LONG BEACH,
THAT WE WERE NOT SUBJECT TO THE PROVISIONS OF PROPOSITION NINE UNTIL THE
MEASURE WE ARE SUPPORTING HAD QUALIFIED FOR THE BALLOT. ACCORDINGLY, WE DID
NOT SEND YOU COPIES OF THE TWO MAILINGS WE HAVE MADE. THE BALLOT MEASURE IS
NOW IN THE PROCESS OF QUALIFICATION.

MAILING #1 WHICH CONSISTED OF A LETTER AND TWO RETURN POSTCARDS WHICH WERE
TYPEWRITTEN AND REPRODUCED AT A DUPLICATING SERVICE WAS MAILED USING OUR BULK
RATE PERMIT NUMBER 42.

MAILING #2 WHICH CONSISTED OF SLIGHTLY OVER TWO HUNDRED COPIES OF A REPRODUCTION OF A NEWSPAPER ADVERTISEMENT THAT WE HAD RUN ADVOCATING FIRST
CLASS POSTAGE STAMPS AND DID NOT CONTAIN THE NAME OF THE PRINTER, DIRECT MAIL
ADVERTISING & PRINTING COMPANY. THIS MAILING WAS MADE ON APRIL 14, 1976. LET
ME CORRECT THIS TO STATE THAT THERE WERE FOUR HUNDRED COPIES OF THIS MAILING
SENT OUT.

IF CONTRARY TO THE ADVISE OF THE LOCAL FILING OFFICER WE WERE SUBJECT TO
THE PROVISIONS OF PROPOSITION NINE PRIOR TO THIS TIME, WE BEG YOUR INDULGENCE
ON ANY VIOLATIONS WE MAY HAVE COMMITTED UNDER APPLICABLE PROVISIONS OF THE LAW
SINCE WE WERE RULING UPON THE OPINION OF THE LOCAL FILING OFFICER.

RESPECTFULLY SUBMITTED,

CITIZENS FOR DISTRICT ELECTIONS

[signature]

JERRY E. STEIN, TREASURER


ENCL: 2 MAILINGS

INDEPENDE

LONG BEAC

IF YOU CARE ABOUT
LONG BEACH,
TAKE 82 SECONDS
OF YOUR TIME
TO READ THIS!

If you believe that the special
interests in Long Beach have
had the run of our City too
long, then join us in changing

MAIL 1

TO THE CITY COUNCIL (
WE, THE UNDERS

Citizens for DISTRICT ELECTIONS
3633 colorado st. 90314
434-7135

Dear Friend,

Voter indifference is an increasing problem here in Long Beach where recent
municipal elections have been characterized by low voter turnout. During the
last City Council race, only 33 percent of those eligible voted.

Among the most commonly prescribed reasons for negative citizen reaction to
government are its ever-increasing size, its impersonal nature and the
difficulty experienced by the voter in identifying and communicating with his
elected representatives.

In Long Beach nine City Council members represent over 130,000 voters and
370,000 residents. Many voters are unaware how costly and cumbersome their
election process is. After district primaries, the two top vote getters in
each district must run citywide. In some cases, the election winner in the
district has subsequently lost in the citywide Municipal Election, depriving
the district of its preferred representative. After election, the City Council
protocol calls for each Council member to keep "hands-off" the other eight
Districts and in effect become a District Council member only. Moreover, the
question of campaign funding continues to trouble political process. Running
both in the district primary and in the Municipal Election in Long Beach vastly
increases the financial burden on the candidate of average means who cannot
afford to campaign throughout the nine districts. In practice, many candidates
can only hope for endorsement by the newspaper as a means of reaching the
electorate. This in turn affords the newspaper, as the only such medium in
the community, inordinate influence over the outcome of all citywide elections,
and possibly over the candidates as well.

I've enclosed a schedule showing the dates on which the reports must be filed with the Long Beach City Clerk. Your letter referred to mailings sent out during the qualification drive. If one uses postage stamps in sending 200 or more pieces of identical or nearly identical mail, the sender must be identified by name and address on the outside of the mailing and on at least one of the enclosures; if the sender uses a bulk rate permit or postage meter, however, the Political Reform Act does not require identification of the sender.
